topic: Flagship generalization of the banked network-exposure partial-ID result: sharp partial identification of complier direct and spillover effects when both the treatment exposure mapping and the instrumental exposure mapping may be misspecified, bridging exposure-mapping robustness with IV noncompliance and producing a computable finite-network sharp bound plus a small worked witness.

open_problems.open_problem: Derive sharp finite-network partial-identification bounds for complier direct and spillover effects when both the treatment-exposure mapping for outcomes and the instrumental-exposure mapping for take-up may be misspecified.

source_refs.claim: Introduces instrumental exposure mappings for network IV with noncompliance and unknown interference, but works through identification and IPW estimation rather than finite-network sharp bounds for joint treatment- and instrument-exposure misspecification.
source_refs.author: Hoshino and Yanagi
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp bounds for direct and spillover effects under misspecified treatment exposure mappings, but not in an IV/noncompliance complier-effects framework.
source_refs.author: Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus

source_refs.claim: Separates exposure mappings as estimand definitions from exposure mappings as identifying assumptions, motivating robustness to misspecified exposure labels but not IV complier bounds.
source_refs.author: Savje

source_refs.excerpt: sharp joint-completion LP bounds for network-exposure ADEs with strict cellwise-relaxation nonsharpness.
Causal inference with noncompliance and unknown interference.

open_problems.why_unsolved: The literature has adjacent but non-overlapping pieces: Hoshino and Yanagi handle noncompliance and unknown network interference through an instrumental exposure mapping, while Schroder et al. and Savje handle exposure-map misspecification without latent IV compliance strata. The parent CausalSmith banked result supplies sharp finite-network treatment-exposure completion logic, but no prior artifact combines IV compliance types, instrumental exposure mappings, and treatment-exposure graph completions in one feasible set.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A finite-network sharp identified interval, expressed as an explicit LP, support-function formula, or finite enumeration with a sharpness theorem and small worked witness, for complier direct and spillover effects under stated misspecification neighborhoods for both exposure maps.

open_problems.open_problem: Identify the exact finite graph-feasibility certificate family for infeasible endpoint assignments in shared-completion network-exposure bounds.

source_refs.excerpt: start with K_i=1 matching/cover certificates before claiming the fully capacitated b-matching case.
Add zero-exposure incompatibility constraints to Conjecture 2's certificate formulation
a violated finite certificate from the associated capacitated b-matching / set-covering polytope

open_problems.why_unsolved: The existing proposal reduces nonsharp cellwise endpoints to hidden-edge feasibility, but reviewer comments repeatedly separate Farkas LP infeasibility, integer infeasibility, matching odd-set inequalities, lifted covers, and zero-exposure constraints. The unresolved obstacle is a precise polyhedral characterization of which signed exposure demands can be realized by a common finite graph completion.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sufficient-and-necessary certificate theorem for hidden-edge endpoint infeasibility, starting with the K_i=1 matching/cover case and extending or explicitly delimiting the capacitated case.

open_problems.open_problem: Prove or refute that shared hidden-edge compatibility makes independent cellwise exposure-misspecification bounds strictly nonsharp for finite-network ADEs.

source_refs.excerpt: The exact minimal number of units needed for strict containment is intentionally left open.
Add the four-unit capacity witness as an explicit numerical corollary or example

open_problems.why_unsolved: The parent proposal states strict containment as an unproved conjectural direction and its honest scope flags leave the minimal witness size open. The reviewer request for a four-unit capacity witness pins the concrete missing object: an observable finite law whose unitwise exposure-error kernels are individually admissible but whose endpoint choices cannot be realized by one shared hidden graph.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A strict-containment theorem or counterexample, ideally with a four-unit finite table showing an endpoint in the cellwise relaxation that violates shared-completion feasibility.

open_problems.open_problem: Characterize sharp bounds for direct and spillover complier effects under two-sided noncompliance with multiple peers, without one-sided compliance or independence of peers' compliance types.

source_refs.claim: Shows that IV rescaling generally aggregates multiple direct and spillover effects across compliance types, with point identification under one-sided noncompliance.
source_refs.author: Vazquez-Bare
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Establishes nonparametric identification of complier average direct and spillover effects in two-stage randomized experiments under stratified interference.
source_refs.author: Imai, Jiang, and Malani
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

source_refs.claim: Provides sharp bounds for direct and spillover effects in two-person encouragement designs with strategic interaction, not general finite networks with many peers and exposure mappings.
source_refs.author: Acerenza, Martinez-Iriarte, Sanchez-Becerra, and Spini

open_problems.why_unsolved: Existing IV-spillover results recover point identification by imposing one-sided noncompliance, stratified cluster exposure, or restrictions on peers' latent types, and the sharp-bound household result solves a two-person setting. The general finite-network case requires a high-dimensional principal-strata polytope where own compliance, peer compliance, treatment exposure, and spillover potential outcomes are jointly latent.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sharp identified-set theorem for network complier direct and spillover effects under two-sided noncompliance, expressed as a finite principal-strata feasibility problem with a proof of sharpness.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop sensitivity-indexed sharp bounds for network LATE-type direct and spillover effects when IV monotonicity can fail through exposure-dependent defiers.

source_refs.claim: Uses instrument validity and monotonicity to identify LATE, making monotonicity violations the classical obstacle for complier-effect interpretation.
source_refs.author: Imbens and Angrist

source_refs.claim: Derives sharp LATE sensitivity bounds under monotonicity violations using defier-share and outcome-distribution sensitivity parameters, outside interference settings.
source_refs.author: Noack

source_refs.claim: Identifies complier and defier LATEs under weaker local monotonicity conditions, but not with network exposure mappings.
source_refs.author: Dahl, Huber, and Mellace

source_refs.claim: Shows that spillovers in take-up and outcomes make ordinary IV contrasts aggregate several compliance-type-specific effects.
source_refs.author: Vazquez-Bare
source_refs.venue: Journal of the American Statistical Association

open_problems.why_unsolved: The study-pipeline defier gaps are hypotheses to validate, and the published monotonicity-sensitivity literature treats no-interference LATE. In networks, defier status may depend on own instrument exposure and peers instrument exposure, so a scalar defier-share relaxation does not immediately determine sharp bounds for direct and spillover complier effects.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A sensitivity theorem giving sharp intervals for complier direct and spillover effects as functions of exposure-specific defier-share and defier-outcome restrictions, including validation or a counterexample for the hypothesized network extension.

open_problems.open_problem: Determine when bounds on misspecified exposure-indexed complier effects imply policy-relevant bounds for interventions that change treatment or encouragement assignments in a network.

source_refs.claim: Argues that exposure effects are not automatically policy-useful, especially when estimands are defined through coarse or misspecified exposure mappings.
source_refs.author: Auerbach, Auerbach, and Tabord-Meehan

source_refs.claim: Defines and partially identifies policy-relevant direct and spillover effects with IVs under interference, including spillovers from instruments to treatment and from treatment to outcomes.
source_refs.author: Nibbering and Oosterveen

source_refs.claim: Recommends separating exposure effects from identifying assumptions, creating a need to connect estimable misspecified-exposure quantities to policy quantities.
source_refs.author: Savje

open_problems.why_unsolved: Misspecified exposure effects may be estimable, but policy interventions operate on assignment vectors or encouragement rules rather than on the analyst-defined exposure labels. IV noncompliance adds a latent channel because encouragement policies affect take-up through own and peer compliance behavior, so exposure-level complier bounds need not automatically bound the policy effect.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A necessary-and-sufficient condition, or a counterexample plus sufficient-condition theorem, connecting exposure-map robustness bounds for complier effects to sharp finite-network bounds on a policy intervention effect.

open_problems.open_problem: Develop inference for estimated sharp bounds on network IV direct and spillover effects when the bounds are computed from finite-network exposure and compliance constraints.

source_refs.claim: Provides confidence intervals for partially identified scalar parameters when the identified region is an interval with estimated endpoints.
source_refs.author: Imbens and Manski

source_refs.claim: Develops estimation and confidence regions for partially identified econometric models with moment inequalities and equalities.
source_refs.author: Chernozhukov, Hong, and Tamer

source_refs.claim: Develops orthogonal estimators for misspecified-exposure treatment-effect bounds, but not for IV complier bounds with latent compliance and instrumental exposure maps.
source_refs.author: Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus

source_refs.claim: Provides randomization-based inference and power analysis for direct and spillover effects in two-stage randomized experiments, not set inference for sharp IV partial-ID bounds under misspecification.
source_refs.author: Jiang, Imai, and Malani

open_problems.why_unsolved: General partial-ID inference handles interval endpoints or moment-inequality sets, and two-stage interference inference handles identified estimands. A computable sharp bound for network IV misspecification would have nonsmooth endpoints from a finite high-dimensional feasibility problem, network dependence, exposure probabilities, and latent compliance constraints.
open_problems.what_a_resolution_would_look_like: A valid confidence procedure for the sharp bound endpoints or identified interval, with assumptions under which bootstrap, randomization, or moment-inequality inversion has uniform coverage.

literature_map:
Hudgens and Halloran (2008, JASA) is the older two-stage interference anchor for direct and spillover effects under partial or stratified interference.
Aronow and Samii (2017, Annals of Applied Statistics) formalizes design-based exposure mappings for general interference.
Manski (2013, Econometrics Journal) anchors treatment response with social interactions and exposure-type restrictions in econometrics.
Leung (2022, Econometrica) weakens exact neighborhood interference to approximate neighborhood interference and gives asymptotic inference under network growth.
Savje (2024, Biometrika) separates exposure effects as estimands from exposure mappings as identifying assumptions, making misspecification a first-class issue.
Weinstein and Nevo (2026, Biometrics) isolates network misspecification as distinct from exposure-map misspecification and notes both may be wrong in practice.
Schroder, Oprescu, Feuerriegel, and Kallus (2026, arXiv) gives sharp partial-ID bounds and orthogonal estimators for treatment exposure-map misspecification, but not IV/noncompliance.
Imai, Jiang, and Malani (2021, JASA) identifies complier direct and spillover effects in two-stage randomized experiments with noncompliance under stratified interference.
Hoshino and Yanagi (2024, JASA) extends to network interference of unknown form using instrumental exposure mappings and considers misspecification, but leaves a gap for sharp finite-network joint treatment/IEM bounds.
Vazquez-Bare (2023, JASA) shows standard IV spillover contrasts mix compliance-type-specific effects and identifies under one-sided noncompliance or peer-type restrictions.
Acerenza et al. (2025, arXiv) gives sharp bounds for two-person within-household encouragement designs with interference, suggesting but not solving the multi-peer finite-network case.
Imbens and Angrist (1994, Econometrica), Noack (2021, arXiv), and Dahl-Huber-Mellace (2023, Econometrics Journal) map the monotonicity/defier frontier for LATE outside network interference.
Auerbach, Auerbach, and Tabord-Meehan (2024, arXiv) challenges the policy relevance of exposure effects, while Nibbering and Oosterveen (2025, arXiv) develops policy-relevant IV bounds under interference.
Imbens-Manski (2004) and Chernozhukov-Hong-Tamer (2007) provide partial-ID inference anchors, but network IV sharp-bound inference remains a specialized open layer.
prior_proposal_map:
Walked the parent upgrade source first: requested parent_tier=accepted, but the available bank entry is CausalSmith/doc/formalization/_bank/candidates/pid_network_exposure_v1, whose README records accepted field-tier shared-completion LP bounds for network-exposure ADEs.
The parent proposal and reviews yielded two core Track-A gaps: strict cellwise-relaxation nonsharpness under shared hidden edges, and the missing exact matching/cover certificate family for infeasible endpoint assignments.
Prior pid_network_iv_exposure v1/v2 failed before producing scientific artifacts; their READMEs explicitly say no proposal or mathematical artifact was produced, so they were not used as evidence.
The failed pid_network_random_set_v1 proposal and rejected angle3 supplied adjacent network-exposure gaps: latent K-support direct-indirect decomposition, nonrectangular witnesses, and sparse exposure-collision/testability frontiers.
Reviewer JSONs for pid_network_random_set_v1 repeatedly asked for quotient definitions, support-function reports, corrected K=2 witnesses, and explicit numerical finite-network witnesses.
Adjacent IV/noncompliance entries pid_ordinal_late_partial_defiers_v1 and pid_dynamic_iv_compliance_v2 supplied compliance-compatibility gaps relevant to the requested IV exposure upgrade: global response-path compatibility and joint mixture-subtraction envelopes.
Cross-track linking merged the parent network-exposure completion gap with the Hoshino-Yanagi, Savje, and Schroder et al. literature gap.
